text: This presentation is for the single port robotic transanal minimally invasive surgery or SB-TAMIS technique. We have no disclosures. The first case will demonstrate the basic principles of this technique. And the second case will demonstrate the capabilities of this technique.

The surgeon examines a 3 cm tubular villous adenoma located 16 cm from the anal verge using a robotic camera. The patient is in lithotomy position with the SP robot docked between the legs. A gel port protects the anal canal, and a single port trocar is used along with an assistant port for CO₂ insufflation. The lesion's boundaries are marked with monopolar electrocautery, followed by full-thickness dissection until the perirectal fat is visible. Instruments are introduced through the assist port for counter traction. The specimen is grasped and removed through the robotic port. The defect is closed using two barbed absorbable sutures transversely to prevent narrowing of the rectal lumen.

The robotic arms are fully deployed and functioning, with their color changing from white to yellow and red if external tension is experienced. The assistant cuts and removes the sutures, and a completion proctoscopy reveals the defect has been sutured closed, showing a comparison to its appearance prior to the operation.

The surgeon operates on a large mid-rectal sessile mass using single port robotic transanal minimally invasive surgery (SB-TAMIS). The mass is located close to the dentate line, with the trans-anal gel port restricting the robotic arms, indicated by the red color in the instrument animation. Despite limited deployment, the surgeon uses serpiginous camera motion for better visualization and marks the margins before performing full thickness dissection. The distal dissection near the anal verge is challenging, but the robotic arms operate precisely in the small area. As the dissection progresses to the proximal rectum, the arms fully deploy, indicated by the animation changing from red to normal. The mass is removed via the assist port, maintaining orientation for potential frozen section analysis. The defect is closed transversely using two barbed absorbable sutures, aided by continuous CO₂ insufflation to prevent colon bellowing.
The video shows a completion view of the sutured defect in the rectum, marking the end of the surgical procedure.
